title: React Fundamentals
description: Everything you need to know to start building real things with React -- explained from scratch with plain language, analogies, and hands-on code examples.

blocks.title: How to use this page
blocks.body: Click on any topic below to expand it. Each section builds on the ones before it, so if you are brand new, start at the top and work your way down. If you already know some React, feel free to jump to any section.

Imagine you are building something with LEGO bricks. Each brick is a small, self-contained piece. You can combine bricks to make bigger things -- a wall, a window, an entire house. In React, **components** are your LEGO bricks.
A component is simply a **JavaScript function that returns some HTML-like code**. That is it. Every button, every card, every page you see on a React website is a component.
Notice a few important rules:

items: The function name starts with a **capital letter** (Greeting, not greeting). React requires this so it can tell your components apart from regular HTML tags.
The function **returns** some HTML-like code. This special syntax is called JSX (we will cover it next).
You can **use components inside other components**, just like stacking LEGO bricks.

blocks.title: Key Takeaway
blocks.body: A component is a function that returns JSX. Name it with a capital letter. You can use components inside other components, just like nesting LEGO bricks to build bigger structures.
JSX stands for **JavaScript XML**. It looks like HTML, but it lives right inside your JavaScript files. Think of it as a template language that lets you describe what the screen should look like.

blocks.heading: Curly braces: the escape hatch to JavaScript
blocks.body: Inside JSX, curly braces `{}` let you drop back into JavaScript. You can put any JavaScript expression inside them -- variables, math, function calls.

blocks.heading: Fragments: returning multiple elements
blocks.body: A component can only return **one** top-level element. If you do not want to add an extra `<div>`, use a **fragment** -- an empty tag `<>...</>` that wraps your elements without adding anything to the page.

blocks.title: Key Takeaway
blocks.body: JSX looks like HTML but lives in JavaScript. Use curly braces to embed JavaScript expressions. Remember: `className` instead of `class`, self-closing tags need a slash, and wrap multiple elements in a fragment `<></>`.

Think of props like the settings on a TV remote. When you use a component, you can pass it information that changes what it displays or how it behaves. The component receives these settings as an object called **props**.

**Strings** use quotes: `name="Alice"`. Everything else (numbers, booleans, objects, arrays) uses curly braces: `age={30}`. A boolean prop written without a value (like `isStudent`) defaults to `true`.

blocks.heading: The children prop
blocks.body: There is one special prop called `children`. It represents whatever you put between the opening and closing tags of your component.

blocks.title: Key Takeaway
blocks.body: Props let you pass data from a parent component to a child component. Define the shape of your props with a TypeScript interface. Use `?` for optional props and destructure them in the function parameters for clean code.

Props come from outside the component. But what if a component needs to keep track of something on its own? For example, how many times a button has been clicked, or whether a menu is open or closed. That is what **state** is for.

Think of state like a sticky note that a component writes to itself. It can read the note and update it whenever something happens. When the note changes, React automatically redraws the component on the screen with the new information.

blocks.title: Important rule
blocks.body: Never change state directly. Always use the setter function. Write `setCount(count + 1)`, never `count = count + 1`. Direct changes will not trigger a re-render and your screen will not update.

blocks.heading: State is local to each component
blocks.body: If you render the same component twice, each copy gets its own separate state. Clicking a button in one `<Counter />` does not affect the other.

blocks.title: Key Takeaway
blocks.body: `useState` gives a component its own memory. It returns a value and a setter function. When you call the setter, React re-renders the component with the new value. Each component instance has its own independent state.

Websites need to respond when users do things -- click a button, type in a field, submit a form. In React, you handle these actions with **event handlers**, which are functions that run when something happens.

**Pass the function, do not call it.** Write `onClick={handleClick}`, not `onClick={handleClick()}`. With the parentheses, the function runs immediately when the page loads instead of waiting for a click.

blocks.heading: Inline event handlers
blocks.body: For short, simple actions, you can write the function directly in the JSX using an arrow function.

blocks.title: Key Takeaway
blocks.body: Event handlers are functions you attach to JSX elements. The most common are `onClick`, `onChange`, and `onSubmit`. Use `e.preventDefault()` in forms to stop the page from refreshing. Always pass the function reference, not the function call.

Sometimes you want to show something on the screen only when a certain condition is true. Maybe you show a "Welcome back!" message only when the user is logged in, or display an error message only when something goes wrong. This is called **conditional rendering**.

blocks.heading: Conditional rendering with early return
blocks.body: For bigger blocks of content, you can use an if statement and return early. This keeps your JSX clean.

blocks.title: Key Takeaway
blocks.body: Use the ternary operator `? :` when you have two options (show A or show B). Use `&&` when you want to show something or nothing. Use early returns with `if` statements for larger blocks or multiple conditions.

Most websites display lists of things: a list of products, a list of comments, a list of search results. In React, you use the JavaScript `.map()` method to turn an array of data into an array of JSX elements.

blocks.title: Why do we need the key prop?
blocks.body: React uses keys to track which items changed, were added, or were removed. Without keys, React has to re-render the entire list every time. Always use a unique identifier (like an ID from a database) as the key, not the array index.

blocks.title: Key Takeaway
blocks.body: Use `.map()` to turn arrays into lists of JSX elements. Always provide a unique `key` prop. Use `.filter()` before `.map()` when you only want to show some items. Chain these methods for powerful data transformations.

blocks.title: 8. useEffect - Running Code at the Right Time

blocks.body: Sometimes you need to do things that are outside the normal "receive data, show it on the screen" cycle. For example: fetching data from the internet, setting up a timer, or updating the page title. These are called **side effects**, and React gives you `useEffect` to handle them.
Think of `useEffect` like a note that says: "After you finish drawing the screen, also do this other thing."

blocks.heading: Basic usage: run on every render
The dependency array: controlling when it runs
Dependency array cheat sheet
rows: Runs once when the component first appears (mounts).
Runs when a or b changes.

blocks.heading: Cleanup: undoing what you started
blocks.body: Some effects set things up that need to be torn down -- like timers or event listeners. Return a cleanup function from your effect to handle this.

blocks.title: Key Takeaway
blocks.body: `useEffect` lets you run code after React updates the screen. Control when it runs with the dependency array: empty array for once, specific values to watch for changes. Return a cleanup function to undo effects when the component is removed.

As your components grow, they can get cluttered with logic -- fetching data, handling forms, managing timers. Custom hooks let you **extract that logic into a separate function** so your component files stay clean and focused.
A good rule of thumb: **keep your TSX files under 50 lines of JSX**. If a component is getting longer, move the logic into a custom hook.

blocks.heading: Naming convention
blocks.body: Custom hooks **must** start with the word `use`. This tells React that the function follows hook rules (it can call `useState`, `useEffect`, and other hooks inside it).

blocks.title: Key Takeaway
blocks.body: Custom hooks are functions starting with `use` that extract reusable logic from components. They keep your components small and focused on rendering. If multiple components need the same logic, put it in a custom hook and share it.

TypeScript is JavaScript with **type checking**. Imagine you have a function that expects a number, but someone accidentally passes a word. Without TypeScript, the code would break at runtime. With TypeScript, the editor highlights the mistake *as you type*.

blocks.heading: Interfaces: describing the shape of objects
Typing props and state in React

blocks.title: Why bother with types?
blocks.body: Types catch bugs before your users do. If you rename a prop or change a data shape, TypeScript immediately shows you every file that needs updating.

blocks.title: Key Takeaway
blocks.body: TypeScript adds type safety to your code. Use `string`, `number`, and `boolean` for primitives. Use interfaces to describe object shapes. Type your props with an interface and your state with `useState<Type>()`.

As your project grows, you will split your code across many files. Imports and exports are how files share code with each other. Think of it like a library system: a file **exports** (makes available) certain functions or values, and other files **import** (borrow) what they need.

blocks.title: The @/ shortcut
blocks.body: In many React projects, `@/` is set up as a shortcut to the `src/` folder. So `@/components/Button` means `src/components/Button`. This avoids messy relative paths like `../../../components/Button`.

blocks.title: Key Takeaway
blocks.body: Use `export default` for the main thing a file provides (one per file). Use named `export` for utilities and helpers (many per file). Import defaults without braces, import named exports with braces.

How you organize your files matters more as your project grows. A good structure means you can find any file in seconds. A bad structure means you waste time searching through dozens of files every time you need to make a change.

blocks.heading: Feature-based folder structure (recommended)
blocks.body: Instead of grouping files by type (all components together, all hooks together), group them by **feature**. Each feature gets its own folder with everything it needs.

blocks.code:
src/
├── app/                    # Pages (Next.js App Router)
│   ├── page.tsx            # Home page
│   ├── layout.tsx          # Root layout
│   ├── dashboard/
│   │   └── page.tsx        # Dashboard page
│   └── settings/
│       └── page.tsx        # Settings page
│
├── components/
│   ├── common/             # Shared across the whole app
│   │   ├── CodeBlock.tsx
│   │   ├── BookmarkButton.tsx
│   │   └── LoadingSpinner.tsx
│   │
│   ├── dashboard/          # Only used on the dashboard
│   │   ├── StatsCard.tsx
│   │   ├── ActivityChart.tsx
│   │   └── useDashboardData.ts  # Hook for this feature
│   │
│   └── settings/           # Only used in settings
│       ├── ProfileForm.tsx
│       └── useSettings.ts
│
├── hooks/                  # Shared custom hooks
│   ├── useLocalStorage.ts
│   └── useMediaQuery.ts
│
├── types/                  # Shared TypeScript types
│   └── index.ts
│
└── utils/                  # Shared utility functions
    ├── formatDate.ts
    └── api.ts

cards.title: Do
cards.body:
Keep components under 50 lines of JSX
One component per file
Extract logic into custom hooks
Break big components into smaller ones
Name files after the component they export

cards.title: Avoid
cards.body:
Files with hundreds of lines
Multiple components in one file (usually)
Deeply nested folder structures
Mixing business logic with UI code
Generic names like "Helper.tsx"

blocks.title: Key Takeaway
blocks.body: Organize files by feature, not by type. Keep components small (under 50 lines of JSX) and extract logic into custom hooks. One component per file, named after the component. If a component is getting big, split it.

blocks.heading: You Know the Fundamentals!
blocks.body: You have covered components, JSX, props, state, events, conditional rendering, lists, effects, custom hooks, TypeScript, imports, and project structure. These are the building blocks of every React application. From here, the next step is to practice by building something real.

blocks.title: What to do next
blocks.body: Pick a small project idea -- a to-do list, a weather app, a personal blog -- and try to build it using only the concepts on this page. You will be surprised how far they take you.

keyPoints: A component is a JavaScript function that returns JSX -- name it with a capital letter
JSX looks like HTML but lives in JavaScript; use curly braces to embed expressions
Props pass data from parent to child components; use TypeScript interfaces to define them
useState gives components their own memory that persists across re-renders
Event handlers are functions attached to JSX elements like onClick and onChange
Conditional rendering uses ternary operators, && short-circuit, or early returns
Use .map() with a unique key prop to render lists from arrays
useEffect runs side effects after render; control timing with the dependency array
Custom hooks (starting with 'use') extract reusable logic from components
TypeScript catches bugs at edit time with type annotations and interfaces
Use default exports for main components and named exports for utilities
Organize project files by feature, keeping components small and focused
